context.project_description: A system for generating structured answers with provenance tracking from knowledge bases, supporting both streaming and non-streaming LLM interactions across multiple provider backends.
key_requirements: The answer generation must support both streaming and non-streaming chat interactions
Answers must include provenance information linking responses to source documents
The system must integrate with multiple LLM providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Cohere, Ollama) through standardized protocols
Answer objects must be serializable and maintain consistent structure across all provider implementations
constraints: All LLM interactions must conform to the ChatProvider and StreamingChatProvider protocols defined in base.py
Provenance data must be included in every Answer object according to the Provenance dataclass specification
The system must support configurable model tiers (e.g., 'base', 'chat') for different provider backends
stakeholders: AI assistant tasked with extracting structured data from analysis
System architect reviewing code for compliance with requirements

in_scope: Extracting stakeholders and scope boundaries from analysis text
out_of_scope: Providing insights about AI assistant capabilities beyond data extraction
Interpreting the purpose or context of the analysis content

existing_files: fitz_ai/core/answer.py — Answer - paradigm-agnostic answer representation. See docs/API_REFERENCE.md for examples.

assumptions.assumption: The file 'fitz_ai/core/answer.py' exists and is readable
assumptions.impact: If the file doesn't exist or isn't readable, the analysis will fail with a FileNotFoundError or PermissionError

assumptions.assumption: The content of 'fitz_ai/core/answer.py' contains valid Python code that can be parsed without syntax errors
assumptions.impact: If the file contains syntax errors, parsing operations may raise SyntaxError exceptions which would break the analysis pipeline

assumptions.assumption: The analysis will extract fields from function signatures and docstrings using standard Python AST parsing techniques
assumptions.impact: If field extraction logic fails due to complex or non-standard code patterns, some fields may be missed in the extracted data

approaches.name: Protocol Extension with Optional Methods
approaches.description: Extend base LLM provider protocols with optional get_last_usage() methods that concrete implementations can implement to expose token usage metadata from API responses.
pros: Maintains backward compatibility with existing codebase
Follows established patterns of optional capability extensions in the system
Enables incremental adoption across all provider types
cons: Requires significant implementation effort across 6+ concrete classes (OpenAI, Anthropic, Cohere, Ollama for chat/emb/rerank/vision)
Each provider must capture and store usage metadata from raw HTTP responses during API calls
Streaming providers require cumulative token counting over chunks
best_for: Systems requiring minimal breaking changes while enabling new metrics collection
Projects with existing protocol-based architecture that supports optional extensions

approaches.name: Engine-Level Aggregation with Metadata Wrapping
approaches.description: Modify FitzKragEngine.answer() method to wrap provider calls and manually extract token usage from API responses using reflection or type inspection, collecting metadata at the engine layer without changing provider interfaces.
pros: No modifications needed to existing provider protocol signatures
Centralized collection point for all LLM interactions
Can work with providers that don't expose usage directly
cons: Inefficient due to duplicated parsing logic across different provider types
Breaks clean separation of concerns by moving usage tracking outside providers
Requires complex reflection/inspection code that's brittle and hard to maintain
best_for: Quick prototyping or temporary solutions before full protocol extension
Legacy systems where modifying core interfaces is strictly prohibited

architecture.reasoning: The recommended approach is superior because it directly addresses the root cause: current LLM provider protocols lack mechanisms to surface token usage metadata. By extending ChatProvider, EmbeddingProvider, and other relevant base protocols with optional get_last_usage() methods, we create a clean, extensible interface that enables providers to expose their actual API response data (prompt_tokens, completion_tokens) without breaking existing code. The InstrumentedProxy system cannot help here because it's designed for benchmarking timing/counts, not parsing return values for semantic meaning like token usage in strings. Engine-level aggregation would be a hacky workaround requiring fragile reflection logic that violates separation of concerns - the providers themselves should know about their own API responses and expose them through standardized interfaces.

key_tradeoffs.protocol_extension_vs_breaking_changes: Extending protocols with optional methods maintains backward compatibility but requires updating all provider implementations incrementally, whereas breaking changes would be immediate but disruptive to existing code.
key_tradeoffs.instrumentation_system_limitations: Using InstrumentedProxy for token extraction is not feasible since it operates on method calls rather than parsing return values, necessitating direct integration into providers.

technology_considerations: Python protocols and typing system for extending interfaces without breaking changes
OpenAI SDK usage tracking capabilities to extract prompt_tokens, completion_tokens, total_tokens from API responses
Anthropic SDK response metadata extraction for token usage information
Cohere API client response handling for token consumption data
Ollama HTTP API request/response parsing to capture token metrics
Pydantic BaseModel for structured API response schema definition
architecture.scope_statement: Update all LLM provider implementations (OpenAI, Anthropic, Cohere, Ollama) to capture token usage from API responses in new optional methods and modify FitzKragEngine.answer() to aggregate and return these metrics via Answer metadata.

adrs.title: ADR: Extend LLM Provider Protocols for Token Usage Tracking
adrs.context: The system needs to track and expose LLM token consumption metrics per query to enable cost monitoring and optimization. Current LLM provider methods return raw content without exposing token usage metadata.
adrs.decision: Extend core LLM provider protocols with optional get_last_usage() methods that can return structured token usage data, allowing providers to capture and expose usage information from their underlying API responses without breaking existing interfaces.
adrs.rationale: This approach maintains backward compatibility while enabling token tracking. It follows the existing pattern of adding optional capability extensions in the system (like caching hooks). All concrete implementations will be updated to capture usage metadata during API calls, and engines will aggregate this data when available.
consequences: All LLM provider implementations (OpenAI, Anthropic, Cohere, Ollama) must be modified to store and expose token usage from their API responses
The FitzKragEngine.answer() method must be updated to collect and include token usage in Answer metadata
Answer structure needs enhancement to support structured token usage summaries
API response models need new fields to expose token usage data to clients
alternatives_considered: Modifying all provider method signatures to return enhanced objects with usage - rejected because it would break existing interfaces and require extensive refactoring
Relying on InstrumentedProxy system for extraction from string returns - rejected because the instrumentation operates on method calls, not parsing of return value semantics

Adding token tracking as a separate cross-cutting concern that doesn't integrate with provider protocols - rejected because it would be difficult to maintain consistency and trace usage across different providers

adrs.title: ADR: Optional Method Extension vs. Metadata Aggregation Pattern
adrs.context: Extending core provider protocols with optional get_last_usage() methods creates a direct interface for token usage metadata, but this approach introduces potential data exposure and coupling concerns.
adrs.decision: Accept the optional method extension pattern despite its implicit dependency on provider-specific API response structures, as it provides the cleanest path to access actual token usage data without requiring middleware instrumentation.
adrs.rationale: The core tradeoff is between explicit metadata encapsulation (which would require defining a separate UsageMetadata type and passing it through all layers) versus direct method extension that exposes provider internals. The chosen approach assumes providers can reliably expose their API response structure, which may not always be true for third-party integrations or when APIs change.
consequences: Providers must implement get_last_usage() consistently across different API responses (e.g., OpenAI vs Anthropic), creating coupling to specific provider behavior patterns
API metadata exposure becomes implicit rather than explicit, potentially exposing internal implementation details of providers in ways that could break if providers change their response formats
alternatives_considered: Implement a centralized usage aggregation system that intercepts all API calls and maintains token counts - this would be more robust against provider changes but requires middleware instrumentation that we explicitly rejected as insufficient
Define explicit UsageMetadata interfaces that must be returned by every provider operation, requiring upstream consumers to handle metadata passing through all call chains

artifacts.content:
from __future__ import annotations
import concurrent.futures
import hashlib
import logging
import pathlib
import re
import threading
import time
import typing
import uuid

from fitz_ai.engines.fitz_krag.config.schema import FitzKragConfig
from fitz_ai.llm.client import LLMClient
from fitz_ai.core.answer import Answer
from fitz_ai.logging.logger import get_logger

class FitzKragEngine:
    def __init__(self, config: FitzKragConfig):
        self.config = config
        self._logger = get_logger(__name__)

    def answer(self, query: Query) -> Answer:
        # This would be modified to collect usage data from providers
        result = "Sample response"
        return Answer(text=result)

phases.name: Read Before Writing
phases.objective: Verify integration points by reading source
phases.verification_command: pytest fitz_ai/llm/providers/test_base.py fitz_ai/llm/providers/test_openai.py fitz_ai/llm/providers/test_anthropic.py fitz_ai/llm/providers/test_cohere.py fitz_ai/llm/providers/test_ollama.py

phases.name: Instrument LLM Providers
phases.objective: Wrap LLM provider classes with instrumentation to track token usage without modifying core logic
deliverables: InstrumentedProxy wrapper for ChatProvider, EmbeddingProvider, and other protocol implementations
key_risks: Incorrectly wrapping methods that don't return token metadata
Breaking existing provider interfaces when adding instrumentation

phases.name: Implement Token Tracking Hook
phases.objective: Create a benchmark hook that extracts and logs token usage from provider responses
deliverables: Global registration of hook during application startup
key_risks: Hook not receiving expected data structure from providers
Performance overhead from logging token usage

phases.name: Update Provider Implementations (Optional)
phases.objective: Ensure concrete provider classes return structured metadata for token extraction
deliverables: Modified OpenAIChat.chat() to include usage data in response dict
Similar updates for Anthropic, Cohere, Ollama providers if needed
key_risks: Breaking changes to existing provider contracts
Inconsistent metadata fields across different LLM providers

phases.name: Aggregate Query-Level Token Usage (Optional)
phases.objective: Accumulate total tokens per query for cost visibility and performance tracking
deliverables: Thread-local storage or context manager to track token sums across a single ask/query
key_risks: Context propagation issues in async environments

risks.description: Token tracking implementation requires modifying provider methods to extract usage metadata from LLM responses, which may break if API response formats change or are inconsistent across providers.
risks.mitigation: Implement fallback mechanisms in token extraction logic and add comprehensive unit tests for each provider's usage parsing.
risks.contingency: If a provider returns unexpected response format, log warning and continue with zero token count to prevent query failure.
risks.verification: Assert that all providers return valid usage data or handle missing fields gracefully

risks.description: Instrumentation using BenchmarkHook may introduce performance overhead during LLM calls due to additional function wrapping and hook execution.
risks.mitigation: Use conditional instrumentation only when token tracking is explicitly enabled via configuration flag.
risks.contingency: Disable token tracking hooks by default and provide opt-in mechanism to avoid impacting production performance.
risks.verification: Assert that benchmark hook overhead is below threshold in profiling tests

risks.description: LLM providers like OpenAI may change their API response format or remove token usage fields without deprecation notices.
risks.mitigation: Implement flexible parsing logic that can extract tokens from various possible field names and structures.
risks.contingency: Fallback to no-op tracking if required usage fields are not present in response, with appropriate logging warning.
risks.verification: Assert that system gracefully handles missing or malformed token data from external APIs

risks.description: Token tracking could increase memory usage due to storing additional metadata for each LLM call and aggregating counts.
risks.mitigation: Use efficient data structures (e.g., thread-local storage) and implement periodic cleanup of old tracking data.
risks.contingency: Implement sliding window or time-based eviction policy for token tracking metrics to limit memory footprint.
risks.verification: Assert that memory usage stays within acceptable bounds during high-volume query scenarios

recommended_contingencies: Implement graceful degradation for token tracking when LLM API responses lack expected metadata fields
Add opt-in configuration flag to disable instrumentation in production environments where performance is critical
Use thread-local storage pattern for efficient per-query token aggregation without shared state contention

phases.name: Read Before Writing
phases.objective: Verify integration points by reading source
phases.verification_command: pytest fitz_ai/llm/providers/test_base.py fitz_ai/llm/providers/test_openai.py fitz_ai/llm/providers/test_anthropic.py fitz_ai/llm/providers/test_cohere.py fitz_ai/llm/providers/test_ollama.py

phases.name: Instrument LLM Providers
phases.objective: Wrap LLM provider classes with instrumentation to track token usage without modifying core logic
deliverables: InstrumentedProxy wrapper for ChatProvider, EmbeddingProvider, and other protocol implementations
key_risks: Incorrectly wrapping methods that don't return token metadata
Breaking existing provider interfaces when adding instrumentation

phases.name: Implement Token Tracking Hook
phases.objective: Create a benchmark hook that extracts and logs token usage from provider responses
deliverables: Global registration of hook during application startup
key_risks: Hook not receiving expected data structure from providers
Performance overhead from logging token usage

phases.name: Update Provider Implementations (Optional)
phases.objective: Ensure concrete provider classes return structured metadata for token extraction
deliverables: Modified OpenAIChat.chat() to include usage data in response dict
Similar updates for Anthropic, Cohere, Ollama providers if needed
key_risks: Breaking changes to existing provider contracts
Inconsistent metadata fields across different LLM providers
